title: Flores Hobbits' eating habits offer clues about their evolutionary past
gist: A Science Advances study argues Homo floresiensis scavenged Komodo-dragon kills rather than hunting pygmy elephants, challenging assumptions about the Hobbits' behavioral complexity and reopening debate over which hominin first left Africa.

insights.claim: Bone-mark analysis shows Komodo dragons targeted the meatiest Stegodon parts while Homo floresiensis stone-tool marks appear on less desirable parts, indicating the Hobbits scavenged leftovers rather than making the kill.
insights.speaker: Kiona N. Smith
insights.so_what: A method (comparing tool marks to predator tooth marks) that overturns a prior 'big-game hunter' interpretation shows how much archaeological consensus rests on reinterpretable trace evidence.
insights.locator: These small hominins weren't big-game hunters

insights.claim: Researchers validated the interpretation experimentally by feeding a whole goat carcass to a Zoo Atlanta Komodo dragon and matching the resulting serrated-tooth marks to those on Liang Bua Stegodon bones.
insights.speaker: Kiona N. Smith
insights.so_what: Actualistic experiments provide the controlled baseline that makes the scavenging inference credible.
insights.locator: To better understand the Stegodon bones

insights.claim: No evidence of fire was found in the Homo floresiensis layers, implying the Hobbits ate their scavenged elephant raw.
insights.speaker: Kiona N. Smith
insights.so_what: Absence of controlled fire is a key data point weakening the case for advanced behavioral complexity.
insights.locator: Veatch and her colleagues also found no evidence of fire

insights.claim: Cost-benefit modeling using ethnographic data ranks Stegodon only 17th among Flores prey species while giant rats rank 9th, suggesting elephants weren't worth the effort even if the Hobbits could hunt them.
insights.speaker: Kiona N. Smith
insights.so_what: Applying return-on-investment logic to prey choice shows behavior may reflect economics, not cognitive limits, complicating the inference.
insights.quote: the costs involved could potentially outweigh any social and/or caloric advantages
insights.locator: And it's hard to say whether hunting Stegodon

insights.claim: Giant rat remains are abundant at Liang Bua in both Homo floresiensis and later Homo sapiens layers, consistent with rats offering a better caloric return than elephants.
insights.speaker: Kiona N. Smith
insights.so_what: The archaeological abundance corroborates the foraging model, strengthening its explanatory power.
insights.locator: And that's probably why giant rat remains are so abundant

insights.claim: Stone tools at Shangchen (2.1 million years) and Xihoudu (2.43 million years) in China predate the oldest Homo erectus fossils outside Africa (1.77-1.85 million years at Dmanisi), implying either erectus is older than thought or an earlier hominin left Africa first.
insights.speaker: Kiona N. Smith
insights.so_what: The tool-versus-fossil timeline conflict is the central unresolved question that reframes human migration history.
insights.locator: But stone tools tell a different story

insights.claim: If Homo floresiensis lacked fire and big-game hunting, it may descend from an earlier, less-brainy species like Homo habilis or Homo rudolfensis rather than Homo erectus, contradicting the view that those species couldn't spread widely.
insights.speaker: Kiona N. Smith
insights.so_what: Reassigning the Hobbits' ancestry would rewrite which hominin was first capable of global dispersal.
insights.quote: Evidence for behavioral complexity in Homo floresiensis, including complex tool and fire use, have weakened considerably over time
insights.locator: The Hobbits and Out of Africa

insights.claim: Despite a smaller brain, the contours of Homo floresiensis' skull suggest its prefrontal cortex may have been similar in size and structure to ours, keeping open the possibility of advanced cognition.
insights.speaker: Kiona N. Smith
insights.so_what: Conflicting anatomical evidence means the behavioral conclusions remain contested, not settled.
insights.locator: On the other hand, despite having a smaller brain
